questions.q: What is the process of issuing debentures?
questions.explanation: The process of issuing debentures involves the creation and sale of debentures to investors in exchange for funds.

questions.q: What is the main difference between a secured and an unsecured debenture?
opts: (A) Secured debentures have a fixed interest rate
(B) Secured debentures have a collateral security
(C) Unsecured debentures have a fixed interest rate
(D) Unsecured debentures have a collateral security
questions.explanation: Secured debentures are backed by a collateral security, whereas unsecured debentures are not.

questions.q: What is a convertible debenture?
opts: (A) A debenture that can be converted into shares
(B) A debenture that cannot be converted into shares
(C) A debenture with a fixed interest rate
(D) A debenture with a floating interest rate
questions.explanation: A convertible debenture can be converted into shares at a predetermined rate and time.

questions.q: What is the process of redeeming debentures?
questions.explanation: The process of redeeming debentures involves the payment of the face value of the debentures to the debenture holders.

questions.q: Why do companies issue debentures?
questions.explanation: Companies issue debentures to raise long-term debt capital and to expand their business operations.

questions.q: What is the difference between a debenture and a share?
opts: (B) A debenture is a loan from a shareholder
(C) A debenture is a loan from a creditor
(D) A debenture is a loan from a bank
questions.explanation: A debenture is a loan from a creditor, whereas a share represents ownership in a company.

questions.q: What is the minimum and maximum number of debenture shareholders?
questions.explanation: A company cannot have less than one debenture shareholder, and there is no maximum limit.

questions.q: What is the main advantage of issuing debentures?
questions.explanation: The main advantage of issuing debentures is that it allows companies to raise long-term debt capital at a relatively lower cost.

questions.q: What is the difference between a registered and a bearer debenture?
opts: (A) A registered debenture has a fixed interest rate
(B) A registered debenture has a collateral security
(C) A bearer debenture has a fixed interest rate
(D) A bearer debenture is issued in the name of the holder
questions.explanation: A bearer debenture is issued in the name of the holder and is transferable by delivery, whereas a registered debenture is issued in the name of the holder and is transferable by entry in the register of debenture holders.

questions.q: Can debentures be issued at a discount?
questions.explanation: Debentures can be issued at a discount, which is the difference between the face value and the issue price.

questions.q: What is the process of conversion of debentures?
questions.explanation: The process of conversion of debentures involves the conversion of debentures into shares at a predetermined rate and time.

questions.q: What is the difference between a convertible and a non-convertible debenture?
opts: (A) A convertible debenture can be converted into shares
(B) A non-convertible debenture cannot be converted into shares
(C) A convertible debenture has a fixed interest rate
(D) A convertible debenture has a floating interest rate
questions.explanation: A convertible debenture can be converted into shares at a predetermined rate and time, whereas a non-convertible debenture cannot be converted into shares.

questions.q: Can debentures be issued to preference shareholders?
questions.explanation: Debentures can be issued to preference shareholders, who have a higher claim on assets and dividends.

questions.q: What is the difference between a cumulative and a non-cumulative debenture?
opts: (A) A cumulative debenture has a fixed interest rate
(B) A cumulative debenture has a floating interest rate
(C) A non-cumulative debenture has a fixed interest rate
(D) A non-cumulative debenture has a floating interest rate
questions.explanation: A cumulative debenture has a fixed interest rate and the interest is cumulative, whereas a non-cumulative debenture has a floating interest rate and the interest is not cumulative.

questions.q: What is the main advantage of issuing redeemable debentures?
questions.explanation: The main advantage of issuing redeemable debentures is that it allows companies to raise long-term debt capital at a relatively lower cost and to manage their debt burden.

questions.q: What is the difference between a secured and an unsecured redeemable debenture?
opts: (A) A secured redeemable debenture has a collateral security
(B) An unsecured redeemable debenture has a collateral security
(C) A secured redeemable debenture has a floating interest rate
(D) An unsecured redeemable debenture has a fixed interest rate
questions.explanation: A secured redeemable debenture has a collateral security, whereas an unsecured redeemable debenture does not have a collateral security.

questions.q: Can debentures be issued with a sinking fund?
questions.explanation: Debentures can be issued with a sinking fund, which is a provision for the redemption of debentures at a predetermined time.

questions.q: What is the process of redemption of debentures?
questions.explanation: The process of redemption of debentures involves the payment of the face value of the debentures to the debenture holders.

questions.q: What is the difference between a registered and a bearer debenture in terms of transferability?
opts: (A) A registered debenture is transferable by delivery
(B) A bearer debenture is transferable by entry in the register of debenture holders
(C) A registered debenture is transferable by entry in the register of debenture holders
(D) A bearer debenture is transferable by delivery
questions.explanation: A bearer debenture is transferable by delivery, whereas a registered debenture is transferable by entry in the register of debenture holders.

questions.q: Can debentures be issued with a call option?
questions.explanation: Debentures can be issued with a call option, which allows the company to redeem the debentures at a predetermined time and price.
